title: Ensure That There Are Only GCP-Managed Service Account  Keys for Each Service Account

input_row.title: Ensure That There Are Only GCP-Managed Service Account  Keys for Each Service Account
input_row.description: User-managed service accounts should not have user-managed keys.
input_row.rationale: Anyone who has access to the keys will be able to access resources through the service account. GCP-managed keys are used by Cloud Platform services such as App Engine and Compute Engine. These keys cannot be downloaded. Google will keep the keys and automatically rotate them on an approximately weekly basis. User-managed keys are created, downloadable, and managed by users. They expire 10 years from creation. For user-managed keys, the user has to take ownership of key management activities which include: • Key storage • Key distribution • Key revocation • Key rotation • Protecting the keys from unauthorized users • Key recovery Even with key owner precautions, keys can be easily leaked by common development malpractices like checking keys into the source code or leaving them in the Downloads directory, or accidentally leaving them on support blogs/channels. It is recommended to prevent user-managed service account keys.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the IAM page in the GCP Console using https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. In the left navigation pane, click Service accounts. All service accounts and their corresponding keys are listed. 3. Click the service accounts and check if keys exist. From Google Cloud CLI List All the service accounts: gcloud iam service-accounts list Identify user-managed service accounts which have an account EMAIL ending with iam.gserviceaccount.com For each user-managed service account, list the keys managed by the user: gcloud iam service-accounts keys list --iam-account= -- managed-by=user No keys should be listed.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the IAM page in the GCP Console using https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. In the left navigation pane, click Service accounts. All service accounts and their corresponding keys are listed. 3. Click the service account. 4. Click the edit and delete the keys. From Google Cloud CLI To delete a user managed Service Account Key, gcloud iam service-accounts keys delete --iam-account= Prevention: You can disable service account key creation through the Disable service account key creation Organization policy by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/orgpolicies/iam-disableServiceAccountKeyCreation. Learn more at: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-service- accounts In addition, if you do not need to have service accounts in your project, you can also prevent the creation of service accounts through the Disable service account creation Organization policy: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/orgpolicies/iam-disableServiceAccountCreation.
input_row.impact: Deleting user-managed service account keys may break communication with the applications using the corresponding keys.
input_row.default_value: By default, there are no user-managed keys created for user-managed service accounts.
input_row.additional_information: A user-managed key cannot be created on GCP-Managed Service Accounts.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/GCP CLI. 
- The audit field provides specific gcloud commands to list service accounts and their user-managed keys, indicating that the verification can be automated.
- The rationale discusses security risks associated with user-managed keys, which directly relates to checking IAM properties within GCP.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_iam_service_account_user_managed_keys_absent

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
1. Use Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud` commands) to list all service accounts: 
   ```
   gcloud iam service-accounts list
   ```
   This command will provide a list of service accounts in the project.

2. For each identified user-managed service account (with account EMAIL ending with `iam.gserviceaccount.com`), run the following command to list keys managed by the user:
   ```
   gcloud iam service-accounts keys list --iam-account=<service-account-email> --managed-by=user
   ```
   Replace `<service-account-email>` with the actual email of the service account.

3. **Pass/Fail Criteria:** 
   - **Pass:** If the output of the `keys list` command returns no keys for user-managed service accounts, it indicates compliance with the control (no user-managed service account keys are present).
   - **Fail:** If any keys are listed for user-managed service accounts, it indicates non-compliance with the control (user-managed service account keys are present).

4. Report the findings on whether each service account has user-managed keys, providing a status for compliance.

5. Use the documentation links provided in the control for any further details regarding service account management:
   - [Understanding Service Accounts](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-service-accounts#managing_service_account_keys)
   - [Restricting Service Accounts](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-service-accounts)
